blocks.text:
Jason Myers
Chief Operating Officer
Roach, Moss and Hall
Dear Mr. Myers:

This letter confirms that {{fact:f:E-2015-001.client}} has retained this firm to conduct a retained search for a Chief Financial Officer. It sets out what we will do, who will do it, what it costs, and the terms we both work under. If anything in it does not match your understanding of our conversation, say so before you sign it rather than after; a misunderstanding at this stage costs a phone call, and the same misunderstanding at offer stage costs a candidate.
Scope of the Assignment

The assignment is a single search: a Chief Financial Officer reporting to you, with responsibility for the finance organization as we discussed. We are engaged on a retained and exclusive basis for this position. We will not run a parallel process for the same seat with another firm, and we ask that you do not either. A candidate approached twice for one job draws the conclusion that the company does not know what it wants, and that conclusion is very hard to talk him out of afterwards.

Our approach is conventional and we make no apology for it. We begin with a position specification agreed with you in writing, because a search that starts from an unwritten idea of the role ends with a slate you do not recognize. From there we map the market, which for a mid-market finance seat means the identifiable universe of sitting chief financial officers, deputies, and divisional finance leaders inside the sectors and geographies you will actually hire from. We approach that universe directly and in confidence. We present a slate of candidates we have met, referenced, and are prepared to defend one by one, with the reasons we excluded the rest written down and available to you. We stay with the assignment through offer, negotiation, and the first weeks after the successful candidate starts, which is when placements are lost.
The Team

Sandra Fuentes, Principal, will lead the assignment and is your point of contact for anything of substance. She leads our search practice, she will be accountable to you for the slate, and she is the person who will ask you what you actually need as opposed to what the specification says. Take the question seriously when it comes.

Jeffrey Patterson, Research Consultant, will build and maintain the market map and draft the position specification. He is thorough past the point most clients think they require, which is the correct trait in the person deciding whom you will and will not see.

James Grant, Senior Consultant, will carry the candidate approaches and the relationships behind them. He is the one who will be on the telephone with your finalist late in the evening when the finalist's spouse has had second thoughts, and he is the reason that conversation usually ends well.

I will not run the search day to day. I do read every slate before it reaches a client, and I am reachable directly if the assignment is not going the way you expect. If you ever have that concern, call me. Do not wait for the next scheduled update, and do not be polite about it.
Fees and Payment

Our fee for this assignment is {{fact:f:E-2015-001.fee}}, fixed at the outset and not calculated as a percentage of the successful candidate's compensation. We quote fixed fees because a percentage fee gives the search firm a financial interest in the size of your offer, and we would rather not have one. The fee is billed in three equal installments: the first on your signature and the commencement of the assignment, the second thirty days later, and the third thirty days after that, without regard to the stage the search has reached. Reasonable and documented out-of-pocket expenses, meaning travel to meet candidates, candidate travel we arrange on your behalf, and any external reference or verification work you ask us to commission, are billed at cost as incurred and itemized on each invoice. Invoices are payable within thirty days of receipt.
Term and Termination

The assignment commences on {{fact:f:E-2015-001.start}} and continues until the position is filled or until either of us ends it. A search of this kind runs about three months from commencement to a signed offer, and we have planned the assignment on that basis. Either party may terminate on fifteen days' written notice. If you terminate, you owe the installments that have fallen due as of the termination date and the expenses incurred to that point, and nothing further. If we terminate, which we would do only if the assignment as defined had become one we could not honestly complete, we will refund any installment paid for work not yet performed. If the placed candidate leaves within twelve months for any reason other than a change in the role or in your organization, we will conduct one replacement search for expenses only.
Confidentiality

Discretion is the whole of our trade. We will treat your organizational information, your compensation data, and the fact of this search itself as confidential, and we will disclose them to a candidate only as far as the conversation requires and no further. We ask the same of you in the other direction. Candidate identities and materials we provide are confidential to you and to those inside {{fact:f:E-2015-001.client}} who need them in order to decide; they are not to be circulated more broadly, and they are not to be used to approach a candidate outside this process. A sitting executive who takes our call is taking a risk with their own employment, and the obligation runs to them as much as it runs to either of us.
Limitation of Liability

We will conduct this assignment with the care and skill customary in our profession. We do not guarantee an outcome and we cannot: the decision to hire is yours, the decision to accept is the candidate's, and neither one is ours to make. Information about a candidate's history, credentials, and compensation reaches us from the candidate and from references. We verify what can reasonably be verified through our customary reference work, but we do not warrant it, and any background, credential, or criminal verification beyond that work is yours to commission. Our aggregate liability under this engagement, on any theory, is limited to the fees actually paid to us for it, and neither of us is liable to the other for consequential, indirect, or lost-profit damages. Nothing in this paragraph limits our liability for our own fraud or willful misconduct, which is as it should be.
Governing Law

This letter is the entire agreement between us on this assignment and supersedes anything said or written before it. It is governed by the law of the state in which this firm maintains its principal office, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules, and any dispute we cannot settle between ourselves goes to the courts sitting in that state, to whose jurisdiction we each consent. Amendments are effective only in writing signed by both of us.
Acceptance

If this letter reflects our agreement, please sign both copies and return one to my attention. We will begin on the date set out above. Sandra Fuentes will be in touch within the week to schedule the intake session with you and with whoever else in your organization will sit across from the finalist, and I would ask that you put the whole panel in one room for it rather than see us separately. Panels that brief a search firm separately disagree with each other later, in front of the candidate.
I am glad you called us. This is a findable role and I expect us to find it.
Very truly yours,

To: Jeffrey Patterson, James Grant, James Weiss
From: Sandra Fuentes
Re: Kickoff, CFO search for Roach, Moss and Hall

The engagement commences on {{fact:f:E-2015-001.start}} and we are retained to deliver a Chief Financial Officer to {{fact:f:E-2015-001.client}}. Our client contact is Jason Myers, the Chief Operating Officer, and the new officer will report to him. I want us clear on one thing before Jeffrey Patterson writes a word of the specification: what Jason Myers asked for in the room and what the job description says are not the same thing. Roach, Moss and Hall has grown past the finance function it built for itself. He described the problem to me as needing someone who can close the books faster. That is the symptom. The objective of this search is a finance leader who can build the function that closes faster and then tell him something useful about what the numbers mean, and if we solve for the symptom we will place someone competent who is gone in two years.
We have roughly three months. That is neither a long search nor a short one, but it is only long enough if the specification is agreed early and the map is not still being argued about in week six.
Workstreams

items: Position specification. Jeffrey Patterson drafts, I edit, Jason Myers approves in writing before any approach call is made. It goes to no one until it is signed off. A specification that changes mid-search costs us every candidate we approached under the old one, and those people do not come back.

Market map. Jeffrey Patterson owns it, and it runs in parallel with the specification rather than behind it. Sitting chief financial officers, deputies, and divisional finance leaders inside the sectors and geography the client will actually hire from. I want the universe defined and sized before we make a single call, not discovered as we go.

Approach and assessment. James Grant carries the approaches and the first conversations. Nothing about the client's compensation goes into an approach call, and I mean nothing, including the reassuring vagueness that people hear as a number. In anything written that leaves this office or circulates inside it, candidates are their seat and their market, not their name.
Client management. Mine. Jason Myers gets a scheduled update whether or not there is news, and he hears anything difficult from me on the telephone first and in writing second.

Scheduling and logistics. James Weiss holds the calendar for the intake session, the longlist review, and the finalist rounds, and candidate travel routes through him. Give him the constraint when you have it, not when it has already broken.

blocks.text: Next Steps

items: Intake session with Jason Myers and his interview panel this coming week. James Weiss is booking it. Hold two hours, not one, and the whole panel at once.
Jeffrey Patterson: specification draft to me within a week of the intake session.
Jeffrey Patterson: first cut of the map, sized and segmented, on the same timetable.
James Grant: nothing yet. No approach calls until the specification is signed. I know the temptation and I am asking you to sit on it for ten days.
All: file discipline from the first day. Every candidate conversation gets a note the same day, in the engagement file, under the seat and not the name.

blocks.text: This is a good assignment with a client who will actually hire, which we do not always get, and I am glad to have the three of you on it. Thank you for making room.
Chief Financial Officer Search: Roach, Moss and Hall

items: Client briefing prepared for Jason Myers, Chief Operating Officer
Search team: Sandra Fuentes, Principal; Jeffrey Patterson, Research Consultant; James Grant, Senior Consultant
Retained assignment, commenced {{fact:f:E-2015-001.start}}
Confidential to {{fact:f:E-2015-001.client}}. Not for distribution beyond the interview panel.

blocks.text: Where the Search Stands

items: Position specification agreed and signed. It has not moved since sign-off and will not without your written agreement.
Market map complete in first pass: the identifiable universe in your sectors and geography, sized and segmented.
Approach calls under way against the signed specification.
It is too early to characterize a slate, and we will not characterize one before we can defend it.

blocks.text: The Market As We Find It

items: The universe that survives the specification is smaller than sector headcount suggests. This is a market of dozens, not hundreds.
Roughly half sit inside firms of your scale or one tier larger. The rest are divisional finance leaders who would be taking a first full officer's seat.
Compensation has moved since the role was last filled. We will bring you evidence rather than impressions.
The scarce trait is not technical accounting. It is the operator who closes fast and can then say what the close means.

blocks.text: How Candidates Are Approached

items: Every approach is direct and in confidence, made by James Grant or by Jeffrey Patterson. No advertisement, no posting, no third party.
Candidates hear the sector, the scale, and the shape of the role before they hear the name of the company.
In everything that circulates, including this document, candidates are their current seat and market rather than their name.
Discretion runs both ways: your search is not discussed with a candidate's colleagues, and a candidate's interest never reaches their employer.

blocks.text: What We Need From You

items: Confirm the interview panel and hold provisional dates now. Calendars, not candidates, are what usually costs a search its best person.
A decision in principle on relocation support, ahead of the slate. Two of the strongest segments of this market sit outside your metropolitan area.
Jason Myers to remain the single decision voice to us. Where the panel disagrees, we would rather hear it resolved than averaged.

blocks.text: Next Steps

items: Approach calls continue against the agreed specification through the coming weeks.
Longlist review, by seat and market, before any candidate is presented to you.
Slate presentation to follow the longlist review, on a date set with your office.
Sandra Fuentes remains your point of contact for anything of substance in the interim.

blocks.text:
To: Jason Myers
Cc: Jeffrey Patterson, James Grant
Jason,

Thank you for the session on Tuesday. It was more useful than these usually are, largely because your people disagreed with each other in front of us rather than afterwards. I would far rather see that now than at the finalist stage.

Since the briefing we have kept the approach calls running against the signed specification. James Grant has had first conversations across the segment we described to you as sitting one tier up, and Jeffrey Patterson has closed the remaining gaps in the map on the divisional side. I am not going to characterize the shape of the slate yet. We are early, and an early read has a way of becoming the client's expectation before it has earned it. What I will say is that the universe is behaving the way the map said it would, which is the first good sign in any search.

Two asks. First, we need your position on relocation in principle, not in detail, before the longlist review. A meaningful part of the qualified universe for {{fact:f:E-2015-001.client}} sits outside your metropolitan area, and I do not want to spend approach calls on people you cannot actually move. Second, please have your office hold provisional finalist dates now. Every search I have lost to a calendar, I lost in the week nobody thought the calendar mattered.

One housekeeping note. Anything we send about candidates goes to you and the panel only, and describes people by their seat. If someone at Roach, Moss and Hall outside that group asks for a copy, send them to me rather than forwarding it. It is not bureaucracy; it is the reason good people take our calls.
More next week either way.
Sandra Fuentes
Longlist Review: CFO Search for Roach, Moss and Hall
Internal working session, firm conference room. Minutes taken by James Grant.
Attendees

items: Sandra Fuentes, Principal (chair)
Jeffrey Patterson, Research Consultant
James Grant, Senior Consultant
Jason Myers, Chief Operating Officer, {{fact:f:E-2015-001.client}} (by telephone, final item only)

blocks.text: Discussion

Jeffrey Patterson walked the longlist. Twenty-two names survive the specification, cut down from something north of sixty, and he took us through the cut rather than the survivors, which is the right way round and not how most people do it. Everyone we dropped, we dropped for a reason he could say out loud. Four came off for tenure, six for scale, and the rest because the close-and-explain piece is nowhere in their history and we are not going to teach it inside a three-month search.

Of the twenty-two, eleven have been approached and nine took the call. Two declined without a conversation, one of them the divisional finance lead we had circled first, which is a real loss. Not moving this year, said so plainly, and did not leave a door open. I had a long argument with myself about going back in three weeks anyway. Sandra Fuentes told me not to and she is right, though I will note for the file that I disagree with the reasoning and not the decision.

Sandra Fuentes then asked what the nine have in common that the other thirteen do not, which stopped the room for a minute. Jeffrey Patterson's answer, which I think is correct, is that every one of them is a single move away from a seat they cannot get where they are. That is not a compliment to our search, it is a description of the market, but it tells us what the approach call should lead with and we changed the opening on the spot.

Compensation came up and we did not resolve it. Two of the nine already sit at or above where we believe the client will land, and Sandra Fuentes will not put an indicative number in front of a candidate before it is confirmed. My view is that we lose people to silence. Her view is that we lose more to a number we have to walk back, and that the ones we lose to silence were never coming. We are doing it her way.

Jason Myers joined by telephone for the last fifteen minutes. Sandra Fuentes gave him the count and the shape by seat and market, no names, and told him where the compensation question sits. He was untroubled by the volume of approaches and troubled by relocation, which he has still not answered in principle; he said he would come back on it this week. He also mentioned, unprompted, that his board chair has begun asking about the timetable, which is worth knowing and was not on the agenda. He asked whether Jim thought the slate was there. I said yes with the relocation answer and no without it, and he heard it.
Action Items

rows: Relocation position in principle, confirmed in writing
Approach the remaining eleven; nothing goes out under the old specification language
Second pass over the divisional segment to backfill the two declines
Confirm the compensation range with the client before any indicative figure reaches a candidate
Candidate file notes current, by seat and not by name

blocks.text: Next working session in a week. Minutes to the engagement file.
Status Report: CFO Search for Roach, Moss and Hall
Prepared by Sandra Fuentes, Principal. Confidential to {{fact:f:E-2015-001.client}} and to the interview panel.
Summary

The search is at its midpoint and on schedule. The position specification is agreed and has not moved since sign-off, the market map is complete and has been extended once at your request, and the approach phase is substantially done. We are assembling the slate now. Nothing has occurred that would cause me to revise the timetable we set at the outset. One thing could, and it is set out under risks below, and it sits with your office rather than with mine.
Accomplishments This Period

The map is finished and has been through a second pass. Jeffrey Patterson has identified and qualified the full universe against the specification and reduced it to a longlist each of us can defend name by name, with the reason for every exclusion written down. That last part matters more than it sounds. A search firm that cannot tell you why it did not show you someone is a search firm that did not look.

Approach calls are complete against the longlist. James Grant has spoken to the large majority of it, and conversion from approach to real conversation is running ahead of what we projected at kickoff. I attribute that less to our persuasion than to the fact that the role is genuinely interesting to people one move below the seat, which is the segment the map told us to work. Of those who engaged, a working group has come through a first assessment conversation and a smaller number are in reference work now.

We also tested the market one tier above the original scale band, at your request and without amending the specification. That work is done and the answer is that it does not help you. At that level, the candidates who would move are moving for reasons that would make them a short tenure here, and the ones who would stay will not move for a business of this scale. I would rather tell you that plainly than quietly show you two of them and let you conclude it yourself in the interview.
Upcoming Work

Between now and the slate presentation we complete reference work on the assessed group, take a compensation read on each of them against your range, and prepare the slate documents. Candidates will be presented by seat, market, and assessed fit, with names disclosed to you and the panel at presentation and not before. James Grant will hold the candidate relationships through presentation and into the interview rounds. Jeffrey Patterson will keep the map live, because a map that stops being maintained at the slate stage is what leaves a search with nowhere to go when a finalist withdraws, and finalists withdraw.
Your office and mine should fix the interview dates now. I have raised this twice and I will keep raising it until it is done.
Engagement Budget

The assignment is being delivered against the fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2015-001.fee}} set out in the engagement letter. That figure has not changed and will not change for the work described here. Two of the three installments have been invoiced on the schedule in the letter. Out-of-pocket expenses to date are modest and consist of candidate travel for the assessment conversations; they are itemized on each invoice and sit well inside what a search of this shape usually carries. The market test one tier up was absorbed within the fee rather than billed as additional scope. We agreed to look, and I would rather not have that conversation with you.
Risks

Relocation is unresolved and it is now the live risk on this search. A meaningful part of the assessed group sits outside your metropolitan area. Every week the position in principle is not settled is a week we are managing candidates whose interest we may not be able to convert, and a candidate who senses a company that has not decided how badly it wants them will find something else to look at. I do not need a policy. I need a yes or a no.

Second, the compensation range. Two of the strongest people in the assessed group already sit at or near the top of the band as we understand it. This is the ordinary arithmetic of hiring above your last officer's market rather than a surprise, and it is manageable, but it is manageable now and expensive at offer. We should have that conversation before the slate rather than after it.

Third, and smaller. Once the slate is presented, the market gives you about three weeks before your strongest candidate concludes that the decision is not really being made. Roach, Moss and Hall has been decisive with us so far and I have no reason to think that changes. I raise it only so that it is on the record before it can become a problem rather than after.

This has been an unusually clear-headed client to work with and it shows in where the search stands at the halfway mark. Thank you for that. I am at your disposal for anything here you would rather discuss on the telephone.
